topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Games / greedy algorithms
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Alice and Ben play the game on a board with $72$ cells around a circle. First, Ben chooses some cells and places one chip on each of them. Each round, Alice first chooses one empty cell and then Ben moves a chip from one of the adjacent cells onto the chosen one. If Ben fails to do so, the game ends; otherwise, another round follows. Determine the smallest number of chips for which Ben can guarantee that the game will last for at least $2023$ rounds.
(Václav Blažej)
response_text:
To determine the **smallest number of chips** Ben must place on a circle of 72 cells to **guarantee that the game lasts at least 2023 rounds**, we must carefully analyze the mechanics of the game and the constraints imposed by the number of cells and the rules of movement.

---

### **Understanding the Game Mechanics**

- **Initial Setup**: 72 cells arranged in a circle.
- **Ben's Move**: He places one chip on each of some cells.
- **Alice's Move**: She selects an empty cell.
- **Ben's Response**: He moves a chip from one of the **adjacent cells** (i.e., the two neighboring cells to the chosen one) onto the chosen cell.
- **Game Termination**: If Ben cannot move a chip to the chosen cell (i.e., no chip is adjacent to it), the game ends.

---

### **Key Observations**

- **Each chip can cover two cells** (its two neighbors).
- **Each round**, Alice chooses an empty cell, and Ben must respond by moving a chip from an adjacent cell to it.
- **If Ben has $ k $ chips**, then he can "cover" up to $ 2k $ cells (since each chip can cover two adjacent cells), but due to overlaps, the actual number of **distinct cells** that can be covered is less than $ 2k $.
- **The game ends when Alice chooses a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.

---

### **Goal**

Ben wants to **guarantee** that the game lasts **at least 2023 rounds**, regardless of Alice's choices. That is, he must ensure that **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip** for 2023 rounds.

This implies that Ben must **cover all cells** in the circle with his chips such that **every cell is adjacent to at least one chip**. If this is possible, then Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip — and thus the game will **last indefinitely**.

---

### **Minimum Number of Chips to Guarantee Coverage**

In a **circle of $ n $ cells**, the **minimum number of chips** required to ensure that **every cell is adjacent to at least one chip** is the **minimum dominating set** of the cycle graph $ C_n $.

For a cycle of length $ n $, the **minimum dominating set size** is:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{n}{3} \right\rceil
$$

For $ n = 72 $:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{72}{3} \right\rceil = 24
$$

So, if Ben places **24 chips** on the circle, he can **guarantee that every cell is adjacent to at least one chip**, meaning that **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.

---

### **Why This Guarantees 2023 Rounds**

- If every cell is adjacent to at least one chip, then **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.
- Thus, the game **cannot end** before all 72 cells are occupied.
- Therefore, the game can last **at least 72 rounds**.
- Since **2023 rounds** is **much larger than 72**, it is **impossible** to guarantee 2023 rounds using this strategy.

But the problem asks for the **smallest number of chips** that **guarantees the game will last at least 2023 rounds**, which is **not feasible** under the current rules.

---

### **Conclusion**

Given the constraints of the problem, it is **not possible** to guarantee 2023 rounds of the game with the given setup, since the maximum number of rounds the game can last is 72 (when all cells are occupied). However, the **smallest number of chips** that guarantees the game lasts **at least 72 rounds** (i.e., when all cells are covered) is:

$$
\boxed{24}
$$

To determine the **smallest number of chips** Ben must place on a circle of 72 cells to **guarantee that the game lasts at least 2023 rounds**, we must carefully analyze the mechanics of the game and the constraints imposed by the number of cells and the rules of movement.

---

### **Understanding the Game Mechanics**

- **Initial Setup**: 72 cells arranged in a circle.
- **Ben's Move**: He places one chip on each of some cells.
- **Alice's Move**: She selects an empty cell.
- **Ben's Response**: He moves a chip from one of the **adjacent cells** (i.e., the two neighboring cells to the chosen one) onto the chosen cell.
- **Game Termination**: If Ben cannot move a chip to the chosen cell (i.e., no chip is adjacent to it), the game ends.

---

### **Key Observations**

- **Each chip can cover two cells** (its two neighbors).
- **Each round**, Alice chooses an empty cell, and Ben must respond by moving a chip from an adjacent cell to it.
- **If Ben has $ k $ chips**, then he can "cover" up to $ 2k $ cells (since each chip can cover two adjacent cells), but due to overlaps, the actual number of **distinct cells** that can be covered is less than $ 2k $.
- **The game ends when Alice chooses a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.

---

### **Goal**

Ben wants to **guarantee** that the game lasts **at least 2023 rounds**, regardless of Alice's choices. That is, he must ensure that **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip** for 2023 rounds.

This implies that Ben must **cover all cells** in the circle with his chips such that **every cell is adjacent to at least one chip**. If this is possible, then Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip — and thus the game will **last indefinitely**.

---

### **Minimum Number of Chips to Guarantee Coverage**

In a **circle of $ n $ cells**, the **minimum number of chips** required to ensure that **every cell is adjacent to at least one chip** is the **minimum dominating set** of the cycle graph $ C_n $.

For a cycle of length $ n $, the **minimum dominating set size** is:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{n}{3} \right\rceil
$$

For $ n = 72 $:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{72}{3} \right\rceil = 24
$$

So, if Ben places **24 chips** on the circle, he can **guarantee that every cell is adjacent to at least one chip**, meaning that **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.

---

### **Why This Guarantees 2023 Rounds**

- If every cell is adjacent to at least one chip, then **Alice cannot choose a cell that is not adjacent to any chip**.
- Thus, the game **cannot end** before all 72 cells are occupied.
- Therefore, the game can last **at least 72 rounds**.
- Since **2023 rounds** is **much larger than 72**, it is **impossible** to guarantee 2023 rounds using this strategy.

But the problem asks for the **smallest number of chips** that **guarantees the game will last at least 2023 rounds**, which is **not feasible** under the current rules.

---

### **Conclusion**

Given the constraints of the problem, it is **not possible** to guarantee 2023 rounds of the game with the given setup, since the maximum number of rounds the game can last is 72 (when all cells are occupied). However, the **smallest number of chips** that guarantees the game lasts **at least 72 rounds** (i.e., when all cells are covered) is:

$$
\boxed{24}
$$
